title: Workflow
text:
The core idea of a robust, reproducible analysis is a clean, repeatable, and script-based workflow. This means that every step of the data analysis is documented in a script and can be executed with a single command. This makes it possible to reproduce the analysis at any time and verify that the results are consistent. The following sections delve deeper into the individual aspects of the script-based workflow:
Parent_Folder/
├── project.Rproj         # Project file
├── data/                 # All analysis inputs
│   └── raw/              # Raw data
├── scripts/              # All analysis scripts
│   └── analyses.qmd      # Main script
├── outputs/              # All analysis outputs
│   ├── figs/             # Graphics and plots
│   └── tables/           # Tables
├── README.md             # Brief project explanation
├── references.bib        # BibTex file
└── licence.txt           # License information


An RStudio Project (file extension .Rproj) is the foundation for a script-based workflow. It can be thought of as the coordination center for the entire project. All files belonging to the same project should be located in the same folder or one of its subfolders. Data can be loaded and saved relative to the project file. This makes it possible to share the entire project folder and verify its reproducibility elsewhere.
To simplify the use of relative paths for loading and saving files, the function here::here() can be used.


Code
# install.packages("here")

here::here("data", "raw", "my_raw_data.csv") # Example path








Note



Project files are also necessary to enable version control via Git and GitHub. You will find more information on this in the following pages.





Raw data is essential for a reproducible and script-based workflow. Raw data refers to materials that could not be recovered if lost (for example, completed questionnaires). Therefore, the first step in a script-based workflow is invariably to create a copy of the raw data, store it under data/raw, and work exclusively with these copies. This ensures that an unaltered version of the raw data can always be accessed if necessary.



As the name of the script-based workflow suggests, the script is the heart of such a workflow. All steps of the data analysis—from data cleaning and transformation to visualization—should be documented in a script. This automatically prevents manual edits and ensures that traceable and reproducible results are obtained. The script connects the (copied) raw data from data/raw and creates the corresponding plots and tables for outputs.






Note



Citations can also be integrated into the script. Furthermore, version control can be managed directly from within the script. You can find more information about this below under “Citations” and on the following pages. Information regarding what to keep in mind within the scripts can also be found on one of the following pages.





The resulting data should be stored separately from the raw data to avoid confusion and to allow for a fresh start with unaltered raw data if necessary. Outputs may include, for example, plots or tables. It is important that scripts are designed to generate these outputs automatically when executed. This ensures that manual edits are prevented and that traceable, reproducible plots and tables are obtained.



As the name of the README file (file extension .md) suggests, this file is intended to be read first when an outside person opens the project. You can think of a README as a manual for the project, which allows third parties to understand the work and build upon it. A README typically includes the following sections:

Project Name & Brief Description: A short overview describing the scope and purpose of the project.
Project Content: A brief description of what is contained in the various folders and how the files are structured.
Instructions for Reproduction: This describes the steps necessary to replicate the results. Reproducibility is often measured by how comprehensive these instructions are and how much prior knowledge a third party requires.
Licensing: If there is no separate license file, the project’s licensing terms can be described here.




When working within a project file, it is possible to integrate citations directly into the script. All you need is a BibTeX file (file extension .bib) containing the corresponding bibliographic entries. This can be created using reference management software like Zotero or manually.
@book{Grinschgl et al. 2026, title={R you Ready}, author={Grinschgl, Sandra, Hirt, Laura and Schilling, Lars}, year={2026}, publisher={R you Ready Publishing} }
If you want your citations formatted specifically in APA 7th edition style, you must first download the corresponding Citation Style Language (CSL) file and save it in your project folder.
The integration of the BibTeX and CSL files is handled within the YAML header of your Quarto script:


Code
---
title: "My analysis"
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
---


In your script, you can then include the citation either directly in the text using @Grinschgl2026 or in square brackets at the end of a sentence.



Licensing of code and data is an important step to ensure the reusability and protection of one’s own work. There are various types of licenses that regulate whether and in what form one’s own work may be reproduced and which requirements must be observed. Depending on the license, a suggestion can also be included on how one’s own work should be cited.

title: The FAIR Principles
text:
FAIR
To make research results more transparent and traceable, there are the so-called FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable). Here is an explanation of what these mean and how we can better implement them through RStudio:

Findable: Software should be easy to find for both humans and machines. Furthermore, software should possess a clearly assignable identifier.
The version of R and RStudio used can be queried using the function sessionInfo(). This allows third parties to understand exactly which versions of the software were utilized. These can then be researched via the official sources of CRAN (for R) and Posit (for RStudio).



Code
sessionInfo()



Accessible: Software should be accessible to humans and machines, ideally via a public license.
R and RStudio are both open source software. The source code behind the R programming language and behind the RStudio development environment is accessible, and it may be modified and redistributed.
Interoperable: Software should be compatible with other software products and data to facilitate collaboration and the exchange of information. This can be achieved through the use of open standards and interfaces.
R and RStudio support a wide range of packages developed by the community. These packages make it possible, for example, to import data from various sources (Excel files, JSON files, files directly from websites, etc.) and to export them again into different formats.
Reusable: Software should be designed so that it can be easily reused by other researchers. This includes ensuring that it is clearly documented, allowing for modifications to be built upon it, or making it integratable into other software.
Files written in Quarto end in .qmd and are therefore also readable by other programs. Packages in R, provided they are listed on the official CRAN website, always include extensive documentation.

Regardless of the software you use, ensure that you implement the FAIR principles as effectively as possible. This way, you can ensure that your research results are transparent and traceable and can be easily reused by third parties.

title: Reproducibility Guide
text:
Ideas for expansion:
Code style: - seeds - Modularisation - Paralellisation - Generation (of plots and tables) - In-line code
Workflow: - Downloadable subsides - Reference to GitHub
Version control: - .gitignore - renv
Other: - Template scripts - container

title: About
text:
About this site


Code
1 + 1


[1] 2

title: Welcome
text:
This is the landing page of the reproducibility guide of the Department Psychology of Digitalisation at the University of Bern. This guide aims to guide and support students in their Bachelor or Master thesis to write reproducible and clean code.
::: callout-note attention Whatever the content of this guide, please note that any guidelines from your supervsior are always superior to the guidelines provided here. This guide is intended to be a helpful resource, but it cannot replace the specific requirements and expectations of your supervisor. Always prioritize the instructions and feedback from your supervisor when working on your thesis. :::
This guide is hosted on GitHub and is open for contributions. If you have suggestions for improvement, please let us know by creating an issue on the GitHub repository or by contacting us directly.
The guide is inspired by the Guide to Reproducible Code from the British Ecological Society (BES).

title: Codestyle
text:
To improve the reproducibility and traceability of analyses, it is important to use a consistent codestyle. A codestyle encompasses the conventions and rules that should be followed when creating code. This not only facilitates collaboration with others but also the maintenance and expansion of the code in the future. In the following, some important aspects of a good codestyle are described.






Note



RStudio provides an automatic formatter that can help ensure a consistent codestyle. This can be activated via the keyboard shortcut Ctrl + Shift + P (Windows) or Cmd + Shift + P (Mac) and selecting “Reformat current document”.




The goal of a reproducible codestyle is to ensure that it leads to the same result regardless of the device used or the person executing it. For this purpose, relative paths are particularly helpful compared to absolute paths. Absolute paths always display the entire path within the file system. Relative paths only show the position relative to the script’s working directory. If the function here::here is used, relative paths are automatically generated based on the location of the project file.


Code
# Example of an absolute path
data <- read.csv("C:/Users/Username/Documents/Project/data/raw/my_data.csv")

# Example of a relative path where the data is stored within the expected data folder
data <- read.csv(here::here("data", "raw", "my_data.csv"))





Variables should always have descriptive names that reflect their content. Functions should include verbs that reflect their functionality. Unless intended, a variable or function should not be named after an existing variable or function.
For R and RStudio, snake_case is used. In snake_case, spaces are replaced with underscores, and uppercase letters are converted to lowercase.






Note



For example, Temp, Data1, or myFunction are not descriptive names and do not follow snake_case, whereas temperature, raw_data, or calculate_mean are descriptive and follow snake_case conventions.


Ideally, files and folders should also be named consistently according to snake_case, and special characters should be avoided. This ensures readability, for both humans and machines, and across different systems.



Comments should be used to explain the code. This is not about the exact logic of already existing functions, which can be traced via the documentation on CRAN. Ideally, comments should provide context as to why specific inputs, functionalities, and corresponding outputs were integrated into the code.


Code
# No comments
data <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)
m <- mean(data)
print(m)


[1] 30


Code
# With an appropriate amount of comments
## Create example vector with measurements
measurements <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50)

## Average calculation
average <- mean(measurements)
print(average)


[1] 30


Code
# Wit an overload of comments
## Defining a variable named 'values'
## The c() function to combine numbers
values <- c(10, 20, 30, 40, 50) ## Numbers from 10 to 50

## Calling the function `mean()` with the argument 'values'
## mean calculates the arithmetic mean
result <- mean(values) ## The result is saved

## print() to output the result in the console
print(result) ## The output occurs here


[1] 30


If a custom function is defined (see below), it should be provided with a short comment explaining the functionality of the function. It should be clear which arguments the function expects and what it returns.
In RStudio, comments or functional descriptions can also be placed outside of a chunk. Inline code is used for this: r:
“The mean of the measurements is 30”.



Long scripts can be divided into several smaller ones. For example, there could be one script for data cleaning, another for data analysis, and a final one for visualization and inferential statistical tests.
Within a script, important meta-information can be provided using the YAML header. This includes not only the author, the date, or the integration of citations, but also formatting options for the rendered document.
Dependencies in the form of packages should be loaded directly at the beginning of the script or, if necessary, installed before loading.
By using the amount of # at the beginning of a line, different levels of headings can be created. For example, a heading of level 1 can be created with #, a subheading at level 2 with ##, and a further subheading at level 3 with ###. This allows the different sections of the script to be clearly structured and demarcated from one another.
In RStudio, settings can be made globally or for individual chunks that influence the output of the script. If these settings are to apply globally, they are added to the YAML header. If they apply only to a specific chunk, the settings are passed directly within it. Here is an overview of the most common options:

eval: true: The chunk is actually executed/calculated.
echo: true: The code of the chunk is visible in the rendered document.
output: true: The results of the chunk (e.g., a table) are shown.
warning: true: R warnings appear in the document. error: false: If the code within the chunk has an error, rendering stops immediately (safe mode).
include: false: The chunk, including code, output, and potential warnings or errors, is completely ignored.

At the end of the script, information about the software used should be provided. The following code can be used for this:


Code
sessionInfo()


Ausserdem kann bei verwendeter Zitation auch noch ein Literaturverzeichnis ergänzt werden:


Code
# This code is usually placed outside of a chunk. Here, the code was only placed inside a chunk because no bibliography exists yet, which would otherwise result in an error message.
::: {#refs}
:::





One of the fundamental rules of good codestyle is not to repeat yourself (Don’t Repeat Yourself, DRY). For this, it can quickly become helpful to write your own small function. The basic syntax for writing your own function is as follows:


Code
# Here the function is defined. The expected arguments are specified in parentheses. 
# The function must be assigned to a name so it can subsequently be called and used.
my_function <- function(argument1, argument2) {
  
  # The function logic is defined here
  result <- argument1 + argument2
  
  # The result is returned here
  return(result)
}


Here is an example of redundant code, where the same plot is defined multiple times with only a different border color:


Code
ggplot(data, aes(x = {{ column }})) +
    geom_histogram(fill = color, bins = 20, color = "white") +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(
      title = paste("Distribution of", deparse(substitute(column))),
      x = deparse(substitute(column)),
      y = "Frequency"
    )

ggplot(data, aes(x = {{ column }})) +
    geom_histogram(fill = color, bins = 20, color = "black") +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(
      title = paste("Distribution of", deparse(substitute(column))),
      x = deparse(substitute(column)),
      y = "Frequency"
    )

ggplot(data, aes(x = {{ column }})) +
    geom_histogram(fill = color, bins = 20, color = "blue") +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(
      title = paste("Distribution of", deparse(substitute(column))),
      x = deparse(substitute(column)),
      y = "Frequency"
    )


Here is a function to create all these three plots in a single code block


Code
"""
The function `create_histogram` creates a histogram for a specified column in a given dataset. The color of the border can be adjusted using the `color_name` argument.

Arguments:
@param data: A dataframe containing the data.
@param column: The column for which the histogram is to be created.
@param color_name: The name of the color for the border of the histogram.

Return value: A ggplot object representing the histogram with the desired border color.
"""

create_histogram <- function(data, column, color_name) {
  ggplot(data, aes(x = {{ column }})) +
    geom_histogram(fill = color, bins = 20, color = color_name) +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(
      title = paste("Distribution of", deparse(substitute(column))),
      x = deparse(substitute(column)),
      y = "Frequency"
    )
}


Lastly the function call with the different colors:


Code
create_histogram(data, column, "white")
create_histogram(data, column, "black")
create_histogram(data, column, "blue")





Another important aspect of good codestyle is clearly defining the code’s dependencies. For this purpose, all required packages should be loaded right at the beginning of the script. This makes it immediately obvious which packages are necessary for the execution of the script. Code can also be written that checks for necessary packages, installs them if necessary, and only then loads the packages:






Important



This code suggestion combines the logic of a custom function and a loop to apply it to multiple objects simultaneously. Please familiarize yourself with the logic before using this code and only adopt the code once you have completely understood it!

Code can also be written that checks for necessary packages, installs them if necessary, and only then loads the packages:






Important



This code suggestion combines the logic of a custom function and a loop to apply it to multiple objects simultaneously. Please familiarize yourself with the logic before using this code and only adopt the code once you have completely understood it!

title: Version control
text:
Fundamentals
To further improve the reproducibility of an analysis, it is useful to use version control. This refers to documenting any changes to the scripts, data, or outputs. This way, it can be traced when and by whom which changes were made. It is also possible to restore previous versions of the scripts, data, or outputs.


GitHub
GitHub is, simply put, a cloud provider like GDrive, OneDrive, or iCloud, but specialized for code and version control. In GitHub, so-called repositories—projects can be created. These are practically the project folder, including all subfolders and files, that were introduced within the workflow. Using the application GitHub Desktop, a folder system on your own device can be connected to a repository on GitHub. Any changes to the files are versioned through so-called commits (!= saving the script). The old files are not simply overwritten; instead, it is recorded which changes exist compared to the old file. After a commit, the change can then be transferred to the repository via push. It is also possible to download the current state of the repository to your own device. This is called a pull.


Git
The combination of a script-based workflow with the version control of GitHub via Git is particularly powerful. Git allows, after a one-time setup and connection to the corresponding GitHub repository, to perform commits, pushes, and pulls directly from within the project file. This eliminates the need for an additional installation of GitHub Desktop and another user interface.






Note



You can find instructions for the one-time setup of Git, GitHub, and the connection between the two here.
